How does hoteling etiquette apply to telecommuting in Canada? Are there any specific considerations for remote work settings? answer this from following paragraph Cell Phone Etiquette Meeting and Presentation Etiquette
Do be on time and be properly prepared by having done any assignments. The big issue today is whether or not to use digital technology such as cell phones, laptops, and tablets during meetings (including during videoconferences). As a general guide, follow the organizational culture and do as the people running the meeting. However, it is considered poor manners to be talking, texting, or surfing the Web while others are talking (this includes presentations); if it’s an emergency, take it outside. Even while videoconferencing, it’s best to dress for business, and don’t smoke, eat, or chew gum
Presentations today commonly use PowerPoint (or Mac Keynote) slides, but don’t bore your audience by simply reading the slides to them-talk to them and connect on a personal level. Keeping the slides to an outline of what you are to present, rather than long detailed text, will help you talk instead of read to the audience.
Networking Etiquette
We’ve already discussed political networking ( Chapter 9): now we remind you to use proper etiquette during the process. Remember to be polite and when asking for help, say please; and affer getting help. say thank you. Don’t ask your boss or people of higher rank to be your Facebook friend or LinkedIn contact, but you may do so if they ask you. Again, don’t do personal social networking on the job

Hotel etiquette and telecommuting have some similarities in that it is important to follow organizational culture and be prepared for meetings. In remote work settings, it is important to ensure your technology is working properly and that you have a designated workspace to avoid distractions. Cell phone etiquette still applies, and it is considered poor manners to be texting or browsing the web during videoconferences. In remote work settings, dressing appropriately for virtual meetings is also important. When networking, it is important to use proper etiquette by being polite and thanking people for their help. However, it is not appropriate to ask higher-ups to be one's social media contacts unless they ask first. Personal social networking should also be avoided during work hours.
does the paragraph have the answer of the question i asked
No, the paragraph does not have a direct answer to the question of how hoteling etiquette applies to telecommuting in Canada.

look at this paragrpah and answer the previous quesion Hoteling, Telecommuting, and Cubicle Etiquette
Note that with the coronavirus and social distancing and the use of mask, there may be specific government and organizational requirements that you need to follow. Here are some general guidelines.
Hoteling is the sharing of workspace and equipment, such as desks, computers, phones, fax machines, copiers, eating areas, refrigerators, coffee machines, water coolers, and so on. Hoteling is common for a mobile organization.™ Do follow the general rule to do unto others as you would have them do unto you, such as cleanirs up after yourself and making sure the equipment is ready for the next person, paying your fair share of any expenses (coffee, bill for lunch, employee presents), not taking other people's food and drinks without permission (return the favor), and respecting others' privacy (don't read or look at things on their desk, computer screen, mail, messages, fax).
Telecommuting is working from off the business premises, usually at home and on the road. Telecommuters usually do at least occasionally go to the work site, so hoteling etiquette applies. The unassigned workspace is called "free address" or "nonterritorial offices.* Do take advantage of the face-to-face time at work and over the phone to network, showing enthusiasm for the people and work.
Telecommuting is working from off the business premises, usually at home and on the road. Telecommuters usually do at least occasionally go to the work site. so hoteling etiquette applies. The unassigned workspace is called "free address" or "nonterritorial offices.*95 Do take advantage of the face-to-face time at work and over the phone to network, showing enthusiasm for the people and work.
Cubicle etiquette refers to working in an open area close to others who can observe your behavior (see and hear you working and your nonwork activities). Try not to bother others by doing things like talking too loud, displaying things (on walls, desks, computer screens) that can offend others, and doing personal things (letting your cell phone ring. applying makeup, clipping nails). Don't eavesdrop. limit chit-chat, and use headphones. Do dress appropriately (end of the last section (14 Appendix A for details), and unless it is part of the organizational culture, don't wear hats (like baseball caps) indoors.

Yes, the paragraph provides some general guidelines for hoteling and telecommuting etiquette. Hoteling involves sharing workspace and equipment, and it is important to clean up after oneself, not take other people's food or drinks without permission, and respect others' privacy. Telecommuting, working off the business premises, also involves following hoteling etiquette when going to the work site. Additionally, it is important to take advantage of face-to-face time and show enthusiasm for the work and the people. Cubicle etiquette refers to working in close proximity to others and not bothering them with loud talking or offensive displays. Dressing appropriately and using headphones are also recommended.
does the paragraph have the answer of the question  i aksed

The paragraph provides some information regarding hoteling and telecommuting etiquette but it does not specifically address whether there are any specific considerations for remote work settings in Canada.

How does hoteling etiquette apply to telecommuting in Canada? answer from following paragraph Hoteling, Telecommuting, and Cubicle Etiquette
Note that with the coronavirus and social distancing and the use of mask, there may be specific government and organizational requirements that you need to follow. Here are some general guidelines.
Hoteling is the sharing of workspace and equipment, such as desks, computers, phones, fax machines, copiers, eating areas, refrigerators, coffee machines, water coolers, and so on. Hoteling is common for a mobile organization.™ Do follow the general rule to do unto others as you would have them do unto you, such as cleanirs up after yourself and making sure the equipment is ready for the next person, paying your fair share of any expenses (coffee, bill for lunch, employee presents), not taking other people's food and drinks without permission (return the favor), and respecting others' privacy (don't read or look at things on their desk, computer screen, mail, messages, fax).
Telecommuting is working from off the business premises, usually at home and on the road. Telecommuters usually do at least occasionally go to the work site, so hoteling etiquette applies. The unassigned workspace is called "free address" or "nonterritorial offices.* Do take advantage of the face-to-face time at work and over the phone to network, showing enthusiasm for the people and work.
Telecommuting is working from off the business premises, usually at home and on the road. Telecommuters usually do at least occasionally go to the work site. so hoteling etiquette applies. The unassigned workspace is called "free address" or "nonterritorial offices.*95 Do take advantage of the face-to-face time at work and over the phone to network, showing enthusiasm for the people and work.
Cubicle etiquette refers to working in an open area close to others who can observe your behavior (see and hear you working and your nonwork activities). Try not to bother others by doing things like talking too loud, displaying things (on walls, desks, computer screens) that can offend others, and doing personal things (letting your cell phone ring. applying makeup, clipping nails). Don't eavesdrop. limit chit-chat, and use headphones. Do dress appropriately (end of the last section (14 Appendix A for details), and unless it is part of the organizational culture, don't wear hats (like baseball caps) indoors.

Hoteling etiquette applies to telecommuting in Canada because telecommuters may occasionally have to go to the work site and share workspace or equipment with others. It is important to follow the general rules of hoteling etiquette, such as cleaning up after oneself, paying one's fair share of expenses, and respecting others' privacy. In telecommuting, the unassigned workspace is called "free address" or "nonterritorial offices." When telecommuting, it is also important to take advantage of face-to-face time and show enthusiasm for the people and work.

Are there any specific considerations for remote work settings? answer this based on previous paragraph

The previous paragraph does not provide specific considerations for remote work settings. However, it does indicate that telecommuting falls under the umbrella of hoteling etiquette. The guidelines for hoteling etiquette, such as cleaning up after oneself, respecting others' privacy, and taking advantage of face-to-face time, may be applicable to remote work settings as well. It is important to note that there may be specific government and organizational requirements related to remote work settings, but this is not addressed in the given paragraph.

Why are table manners important in Canadian business culture? How might poor table manners impact professional opportunities? answer and take lines from following paragraph Do be on time and be properly prepared by having done any assignments. The big issue today is whether or not to use digital technology such as cell phones, laptops, and tablets during meetings (including during videoconferences). As a general guide, follow the organizational culture and do as the people running the meeting. However, it is considered poor manners to be talking, texting, or surfing the Web while others are talking (this includes presentations): if it's an emergency, take it outside. Even while videoconferencing, it's best to dress for business, and don't smoke, eat, or chew gum.
Presentations today commonly use PowerPoint (or Mac Keynote) slides, but don't bore your audience by simply reading the slides to them-talk to them and connect on a personal level. Keeping the slides to an outline of what you are to present, rather than long detailed text, will help you talk instead of read to the audienge.

The paragraph does not provide information on table manners or their importance in Canadian business culture. It mainly talks about general meeting etiquette, such as being on time and prepared, avoiding surfacing the web during presentations, dressing appropriately, and refraining from smoking, eating or chewing gum. It also highlights the importance of engaging with the audience during presentations and keeping the slides simple and focused. While these factors can have a significant impact on one's professional opportunities, they do not directly address the impact of poor table manners.
does this paragraph not have any answer of the question i asked

Yes, that's correct. The paragraph does not directly answer the question of why table manners are important in Canadian business culture or how poor table manners might impact professional opportunities.

Why are table manners important in Canadian business culture? How might poor table manners impact professional opportunities? answer from following paragraph Dining Etiquette
Table manners are important for some jobs because business is sometimes transacted during a meal. In fact, some managers will take the job candidate out to eat and observe etiquette, including table manners. Candidates with poor etiquette are not offered the job, or the business deal. Common etiquette is for the person inviting the other party out to eat to pay the bill. Nina Zagat, cofounder of the Zagat Survey restaurant guide, says: "When ordering food, you are not trying to draw attention to yourself or what you are eating." So avoid food that is messy, such as ribs and lobster. As a general guide, follow the lead of the other person's etiquette.°3 For more details on table manners, see ° Exhibit A.9. The following are a few simple tips in case you are taken out to eat during the job interview or business meeting. If you get the job and take others out to eat, you are in the interviewer role, even if it's not a job interview. Many of the tips also apply to eating with others during your lunch breaks.
Don't be starving when you go out to eat. Pigging out is not appropriate behavior and will not make a good impression on the
interviewer.
.Do follow the lead of the interviewer: don't take charge.
. Do let the interviewer sit first.
Page 51
. Do place your napkin on your lap after the interviewer does.
.If the server asks if you want a drink, do wait for the interviewer to respond. Don't ask for alcohol if you are underage.
•Don't order alcohol unless asked if you want a drink by the interviewer. Iasked, ask the interviewer if he or she will be having a drink. If the interviewer say ves. have one: if the answer is no, don't have a drink. However, don't have a drink if you will feel its effects. You want to be in top form for the interview questions and discussion, and you want to maintain your proper etiquette,
• Do expect to order an appetizer, main course, and dessert. However. you don't have to order them all. especially if the interviewer does not. For example, if the interviewer asks if you would like an appetizer or a dessert. ask the interviewer if he or she is having one. If the server asks, wait for the interviewer to answer

Table manners are important in Canadian business culture because business transactions are sometimes conducted during meals, including job interviews and business meetings. Poor etiquette can leave a bad impression on the interviewer and may impact an individual's chances of securing the job or business deal. Dining etiquette includes following the lead of the other person's etiquette, not ordering messy foods, and waiting for the interviewer to sit and respond to drink orders. It is also important not to pig out or order alcohol without first being asked. These tips are important for job interviews or business meetings, and they also apply to lunch breaks. Poor table manners can impact professional opportunities because they reflect on an individual's level of professionalism and can create a negative impression.

"In fact, some managers will take the job candidate out to eat and observe etiquette, including table manners."

"Candidates with poor etiquette are not offered the job, or the business deal."

"As a general guide, follow the lead of the other person's etiquette."

"So avoid food that is messy, such as ribs and lobster."

"Don't be starving when you go out to eat. Pigging out is not appropriate behavior and will not make a good impression on the interviewer."

"Poor etiquette can leave a bad impression on the interviewer and may impact an individual's chances of securing the job or business deal."

"Dining etiquette includes following the lead of the other person's etiquette, not ordering messy foods, and waiting for the interviewer to sit and respond to drink orders."

"Poor table manners can impact professional opportunities because they reflect an individual's level of professionalism and can create a negative impression."

According to Exhibit A.11, what are some important guidelines for composing professional emails in terms of language, length, and subject lines? answer and take lines from following paragraph EXHIBIT A11 E-Mail Etiquette
Do use complete sentences that are not filled with acronyms people may not understand. Remember that an e-mail is different from a
less formal text/IM, and don't use all CAPS.
Do keep it short and to the point: the best e-mail replies involve one word--Yes. Remember to let the person know what type of
response vou want.
Do proofread your e-mail and spell and grammar check it before sending. Remember that your mistakes are in writing for the world to see, so don't embarrass yourself with poor writing.
• Do use a good but brief description of what the e-mail is about in the subject line.
-Do use the recipient's name in the greeting and sign your name. and be polite (please and thank you).
Don't send needless e-mails, including CCing others who don't need to know your message.
Don't send e-mails when you are highly emotional, especially angry, as it is easy to write things you will regret later: employces have been reprimanded or fired over inappropriate e-mails. So criticize ( Chapter 4) and resolve conflicts ( Chapter 5) in person. when possible: if not, at least be calm. •Do assume your e-mail will be forwarded, and that it could be read in court. It is generally better not to send confidential information in an e-mail to ensure that it is not forwarded to an unintended person. Hopefully, you will not be doing anything unethical or illegal, or at least not in writing. Also, remember that deleted e-mails can be retrieved.
Don't get addicted to constantly checking your phone, e-mail, and text/IM, unless it is required by your job. Recall that multitasking and your phone actually make you less productive.

According to Exhibit A.11, some important guidelines for composing professional emails include using complete sentences that are not filled with acronyms or in all CAPS, keeping the email short and to the point, proofreading for errors, and using a good but brief description of what the email is about in the subject line. It is also important to use the recipient's name in the greeting, be polite, and sign one's name. Needless e-mails should be avoided, and it is crucial not to send e-mails when highly emotional, especially angry, as it can lead to regret and negative consequences. Additionally, one should assume their email will be forwarded and avoid sending confidential information in an email to prevent it from being forwarded to unintended recipients.
